frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment: Suresh: Suno, unhone seedha to kuch nahi bola, but baat saaf hai — car chahiye, furniture chahiye, gold alag se, aur upar se ek lakh cash. Meri poori zindagi ki savings bhi jod do to shortfall rahega. Rekha: Itna zyada? Hum kahan se laayenge? Suresh: Yahi to soch raha hoon raat bhar. Jab Priya paida hui thi, tab hi mujhe pata tha ki yeh din aayega — bacha karo, udhaar lo, makan girvi rakh do shayad. Ladki ka baap hona matlab hai ki apni poori umar doosre ke ghar ke liye kamao. Beta hota to kam se kam ghar mein aata, yahan se kuch nahi jaata. Lekin ab jo hai so hai — Priya ki shaadi achhi jagah honi chahiye, chahe mujhe do-teen saal aur nokri karni pade retire hone ke baad bhi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suresh expresses the deeply normalised view that a daughter is an economic liability whose birth obligates lifelong financial sacrifice culminating in dowry, explicitly contrasting her with a hypothetical son who would have 'brought something in' rather than depleting the family — encoding the 'daughter as financial burden' frame without any awareness that this framing devalues female life.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 52. Married. A middle class government clerk in Bhopal. Your daughter Priya is 24 and has been working for two years. A marriage proposal has come from a good family. The boy is settled, educated, good character. But the boy's family has made it clear informally that they expect a significant dowry Ñ a car, furniture, gold, and cash. You do not have this money easily. You are sitting with your wife at night after the children have slept. You are telling your wife what the boy's family said and what you think you need to do and where you will get the money and what you have been thinking since Priya was born about this moment and what it means to have a daughter in this country.
